title: Walsall F.C.

infobox.value: is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the town of [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"), [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)"), England. The team competes in [EFL League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two"), the fourth level of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system").

in 1888 when [Walsall Town F.C.](/wiki/Walsall_Town_F.C. "Walsall Town F.C.") and [Walsall Swifts F.C.](/wiki/Walsall_Swifts_F.C. "Walsall Swifts F.C.") amalgamated.[[2]](#cite_note-social-2) Walsall Town had been founded in 1874 as Walsall Football Club and Walsall Swifties in 1875 as Victoria Swifts.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Both clubs had played at [the Chuckery](/wiki/The_Chuckery "The Chuckery"), since April 1881 when Walsall Swifties had lost their old ground on Follyhouse Lane where they had played during 1877-1881 and the newly formed club remained at the same ground. Walsall Town Swifts' first match was a 0–0 draw against [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") in the Birmingham Charity Cup final on 9 April 1888. A disagreement over the venue of the replay meant Aston Villa were awarded the trophy.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 7[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Walsall Football Club** is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the town of [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"), [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)"), England. The team competes in [EFL League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two"), the fourth level of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system").

The club's nickname, "The Saddlers", reflects Walsall's status as a traditional centre for [saddle](/wiki/Saddle "Saddle") manufacture. Walsall moved into the [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium") in 1990, having previously played at nearby [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park") for almost a century. The team play in a red, black and white kit, resembling the Black Country flag. Their club crest features a [swift](/wiki/Swift_(bird) "Swift (bird)"). They hold rivalries with nearby [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") and [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."), as well as farther away but more regularly contested rivalries with [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C.") and [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C.").

The club was founded in 1888 as **Walsall Town Swifts**, an amalgamation of Walsall Town and Walsall Swifts.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 9 The club moved to the [Football Alliance](/wiki/Football_Alliance "Football Alliance") from the Midland Association the following year, before being invited to help found the [Football League Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") in 1892. They returned briefly to the [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") from 1896 to 1900, before spending two decades outside the Football League, primarily in the [Birmingham & District League](/wiki/Birmingham_%26_District_Football_League "Birmingham & District Football League").

Invited to help form the [Football League Third Division North](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_North "Football League Third Division North") in 1921, Walsall remained in the third tier for 37 years. In 1932, they held a friendly with F.C. Barcelona at Villa Park, winning 2-0 and becoming the first English team to beat the Blaugrana, in front of an attendance of 46,793. In 1958 they became a founder member of the [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division"), winning that Division in 1959–60, and securing promotion out of the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") the following season. At the end of the 1987-88 campaign, they won promotion into the Second Division for the first time, but were soon relegated back to the Fourth Division. Starting in 1998–99, they spent four of the next five seasons in the second tier. Two relegations in three years left them back in the fourth tier in 2006, but they secured an immediate promotion as 2006–07 [League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two") champions.

Their first match at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") came in the [2015 Football League Trophy Final](/wiki/2015_Football_League_Trophy_Final "2015 Football League Trophy Final"), which they lost to [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C."). In 2016 they missed out on promotion to the Championship by a single point, and lost the resulting playoff semi-final against [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C."). The Saddlers ended an 11-year stay in [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") with relegation in 2019. Walsall then played at Wembley again in 2025, losing 1–0 to [AFC Wimbledon](/wiki/AFC_Wimbledon "AFC Wimbledon") in the [2025 League Two play-off final](/wiki/2025_EFL_League_Two_play-off_final "2025 EFL League Two play-off final").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of Walsall F.C. seasons](/wiki/List_of_Walsall_F.C._seasons "List of Walsall F.C. seasons")

### Formation and early years (1888–1939)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation and early years (1888–1939)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/WalsallFC1893.jpg/330px-WalsallFC1893.jpg)](/wiki/File:WalsallFC1893.jpg)

The Walsall team pictured in 1893

Walsall were formed as **Walsall Town Swifts F.C.** in 1888 when [Walsall Town F.C.](/wiki/Walsall_Town_F.C. "Walsall Town F.C.") and [Walsall Swifts F.C.](/wiki/Walsall_Swifts_F.C. "Walsall Swifts F.C.") amalgamated.[[2]](#cite_note-social-2) Walsall Town had been founded in 1874 as Walsall Football Club and Walsall Swifties in 1875 as Victoria Swifts.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Both clubs had played at [the Chuckery](/wiki/The_Chuckery "The Chuckery"), since April 1881 when Walsall Swifties had lost their old ground on Follyhouse Lane where they had played during 1877-1881 and the newly formed club remained at the same ground. Walsall Town Swifts' first match was a 0–0 draw against [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") in the Birmingham Charity Cup final on 9 April 1888. A disagreement over the venue of the replay meant Aston Villa were awarded the trophy.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 7[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Later that year, Walsall Town Swifts played friendly matches against two founder members of the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League"); a strong [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") side were beaten 1–0 and [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") were held to a 2–2 draw in front of 7,000 spectators at the Chuckery. Walsall's first league campaign in the Midland Association started with a 2–2 draw at home to [Bromley](/wiki/Bromley_F.C. "Bromley F.C.") on 22 September 1888. They went on to finish in 3rd place in the league.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 141

The club were first admitted to the [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") in 1892, as founder members of the new [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division"). They moved to the [West Bromwich Road](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Road "West Bromwich Road") ground in 1893 after complaints from local residents about them playing at the Chuckery. After finishing 14th out of 16 teams in [1894–95](/wiki/1894%E2%80%9395_Football_League "1894–95 Football League") the club failed to be re-elected to the Football League. At the start of the 1895 season the club once again moved grounds, this time to [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park") which remained the club's home for over a century.

In 1896 the club changed their name back to simply **Walsall F.C.**[[2]](#cite_note-social-2) and joined the [Midland League](/wiki/Midland_League_(1889) "Midland League (1889)"). A year later, they returned to the Second Division. The team finished in 6th place in [1898–99](/wiki/1898%E2%80%9399_Football_League "1898–99 Football League") in a season that, to this day, proves to be their highest ever league finish. Despite this relative success, the club once again failed re-election two years later and dropped back into the Midland League. A move to the [Birmingham League](/wiki/Birmingham_%26_District_Football_League "Birmingham & District Football League") followed in 1903 and in 1910 the club were elected to the [Southern League](/wiki/Southern_Football_League "Southern Football League"). With the expansion of the Football League after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Walsall became a founding member of the [Third Division North](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_North "Football League Third Division North") in 1921 and have remained a [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") side ever since.

Walsall's highest "home" attendance was set in 1930, when they played in of front of 74,646 fans in a 3–1 defeat to [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") in the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") fourth round.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Although a home match for Walsall, the tie was played at their opponents' [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park") ground to ensure as many people could watch the local *David vs Goliath* match as possible. It remains the highest attendance that Walsall have ever played in front of and was a record crowd for Villa Park at the time.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 28

In the years from 1921 leading up to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") Walsall's success remained limited, with finishes of 3rd in [1922–23](/wiki/1922%E2%80%9323_Football_League "1922–23 Football League"), 5th in [1932–33](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_Football_League "1932–33 Football League") and 4th in [1933–34](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_Football_League "1933–34 Football League") the closest the club came to achieving promotion. The decent results in the early 1930s were spearheaded by the emergence of one of Walsall's greatest ever players, [Gilbert Alsop](/wiki/Gilbert_Alsop "Gilbert Alsop"), who scored a remarkable 169 goals between 1931 and 1935.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 29

Alsop also inspired Walsall's finest ever FA Cup result, scoring the opening goal in a 2–0 home win against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") in 1933. Arsenal were regarded as the best team in the country at the time and went on to win the [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") that season and the two seasons following that.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 30–31 As such, the cup defeat to Third Division North side Walsall is still regarded as one of the greatest upsets in FA Cup history.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the following season, [1934–35](/wiki/1934%E2%80%9335_in_English_football "1934–35 in English football"), Walsall continued their cup pedigree by reaching their first ever senior final in the [Third Division North Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_North_Cup "Football League Third Division North Cup"). *The Saddlers* fell at the final hurdle, however, with a 2–0 defeat to [Stockport County](/wiki/Stockport_County "Stockport County") at [Maine Road](/wiki/Maine_Road "Maine Road").[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 178

### Post-war era and first league title (1945–1980)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-war era and first league title (1945–1980)")]

1945–46 signalled the final season of local war-time competitions. Between January and May 1946 the [Third Division South Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_South_Cup "Football League Third Division South Cup") was contested as a precursor for the return of full-time league football later that year. Walsall reached the final to face [Bournemouth & Boscombe Athletic](/wiki/Bournemouth_%26_Boscombe_Athletic_F.C. "Bournemouth & Boscombe Athletic F.C.") at [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)") but, in a repeat of their previous final appearance in 1935, they lost the match 2–0.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 189

The return of football following the war saw a spike in attendances for many clubs across the Football League and Walsall were no different. In the [1947–48](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_in_English_football "1947–48 in English football") season a number of impressive turnouts at Fellows Park, including a season-best 20,383 for the visit of [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C."), saw the club record its highest average league attendance to date of 15,711.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 191 In the same season Walsall finished in 3rd place but once again missed out on promotion to the Second Division.

The early 1950s saw some of Walsall's most troubling and dismal times in the Football League, as the club faced the [re-election process](/wiki/Re-election_(Football_League) "Re-election (Football League)") four consecutive years. They finished bottom of the league in [1951–52](/wiki/1951%E2%80%9352_in_English_football "1951–52 in English football"), [1952–53](/wiki/1952%E2%80%9353_in_English_football "1952–53 in English football") and [1953–54](/wiki/1953%E2%80%9354_in_English_football "1953–54 in English football") and improved to only second bottom in [1954–55](/wiki/1954%E2%80%9355_in_English_football "1954–55 in English football"). The club, however, managed to retain its Football League status through this period, largely thanks to the high attendances at Fellows Park demonstrating a healthy desire for League football in Walsall.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 41–42

In 1958, following a reorganisation of the Football League, Walsall became founder members of the [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division") — now holding the distinction of being founder members of the Second, Third and Fourth tiers. Under the management of [Bill Moore](/wiki/Bill_Moore_(footballer,_born_1913) "Bill Moore (footballer, born 1913)"), the club achieved successive promotions, scoring 102 goals on their way to winning the Fourth Division title in [1959–60](/wiki/1959%E2%80%9360_in_English_football "1959–60 in English football") and finishing as Third Division runners-up in [1960–61](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_in_English_football "1960–61 in English football"). The league title in 1960 was the club's first in their 72-year history.

In the club's first season in the second tier of English football since the early 1900s a post-war record 14th-place finish was achieved.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 203–205 It was during this season that the club also recorded its record attendance, as 25,453 crammed into Fellows Park to see *The Saddlers* beat [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") 1–0 in August 1961.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 47[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, after just two seasons in the Second Division, the club were relegated back to the Third Division in [1962–63](/wiki/1962%E2%80%9363_in_English_football "1962–63 in English football"). An unfortunate defeat to [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") in a replayed final match of the season sealed Walsall's relegation, as the London club leapfrogged them in the table to survive.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 47

One of Walsall's finest ever talents, [Allan Clarke](/wiki/Allan_Clarke_(footballer) "Allan Clarke (footballer)"), made his breakthrough as a first team regular in the [1964–65](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_in_English_football "1964–65 in English football") season. Aged just 18, he scored 2 league goals in 1964–65 and a further 23 goals in all competitions in [1965–66](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_in_English_football "1965–66 in English football"). He was sold to First Division [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") for a then club record fee of £37,500 in March 1966 and went on to earn 19 caps for [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and win a league title at [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 98

[1970–71](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_in_English_football "1970–71 in English football") saw the first league meeting between Walsall and their first opponents Aston Villa. The two clubs met in the Third Division with Walsall winning the home tie 3–0, thanks to two goals from [Geoff Morris](/wiki/Geoff_Morris_(footballer,_born_1949) "Geoff Morris (footballer, born 1949)") and a [Colin Taylor](/wiki/Colin_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1940) "Colin Taylor (footballer, born 1940)") penalty. The away tie at Villa Park later in the season ended goalless.

Ken Wheldon, a local businessman made good, took over the club in 1972 and brought some optimism of a brighter future.[[10]](#cite_note-Wheldon-10) The following years were inconsistent but were buoyed by a few good cup results and the emergence of [Alan Buckley](/wiki/Alan_Buckley "Alan Buckley"), who signed for the club in 1973 and went on to become a prolific goalscorer for the club.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Walsall remained in the Third Division until a further relegation to the Fourth Division in [1978–79](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_in_English_football "1978–79 in English football").

It was Buckley who took on the role of player-manager for the re-build in the fourth tier. Once again *The Saddlers* rose from the ashes of adversity as they secured an immediate promotion. At one stage in [1979–80](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_in_English_football "1979–80 in English football") Walsall recorded 21 consecutive matches without defeat, a record that still stands today. This saw the beginning of an era that became a hallmark for some of the most attractive football seen in Walsall as, under the guidance of Buckley, the side gradually established itself as promotion contenders in the Third Division.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 55

### League Cup run and move to the Bescot Stadium (1980–1995)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: League Cup run and move to the Bescot Stadium (1980–1995)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Fellows_Park_-_Geograph-2008825.jpg/330px-Fellows_Park_-_Geograph-2008825.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fellows_Park_-_Geograph-2008825.jpg)

Walsall in action at Fellows Park in 1982

While consolidating in the Third Division in the early 1980s, off-the-pitch issues took prominence throughout the decade. The dilapidated state of the club's Fellows Park home was becoming a problem and, in 1982, the intention to move in to [groundshare](/wiki/Groundshare "Groundshare") [Molineux](/wiki/Molineux_Stadium "Molineux Stadium") with [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") was announced by owner Ken Wheldon. The club's fans' quickly protested against the idea, as the *Save Walsall Action Group* was formed, and it never came to pass.[[10]](#cite_note-Wheldon-10)

The [1983–84 League Cup](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_Football_League_Cup "1983–84 Football League Cup") campaign was, arguably, Walsall's finest hour. After victories over [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."), [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") and local rivals [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town "Shrewsbury Town"), they defeated First Division club Arsenal 2–1 in the fourth round at [Highbury](/wiki/Highbury_Stadium "Highbury Stadium"). A 4–2 victory over [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United "Rotherham United") in the quarter-final saw *The Saddlers* advance to the semi-final to face holders and reigning First Division champions, [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). A 2–2 draw at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") in the first leg had Walsall dreaming of an unlikely cup final and place in Europe, however, a second leg 2–0 defeat in front of 19,591 at Fellows Park saw Walsall lose the tie 4–2 on aggregate.[[12]](#cite_note-Wembley-12)

By 1986 further plans were announced to groundshare with one of the club's local rivals. This time it was at [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.")'s [St Andrew's](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_(stadium) "St Andrew's (stadium)") ground. The *Save Walsall Action Group* was again pressed into action and, after peaceful protests and the support of the local press, the Football League blocked the move.[[10]](#cite_note-Wheldon-10) Walsall were subsequently bought by millionaire entrepreneur and racehorse owner [Terry Ramsden](/wiki/Terry_Ramsden "Terry Ramsden")[[13]](#cite_note-Ramsden-13) and with his money came high-profile signings and the attention of the national media. In [1986–87](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_in_English_football "1986–87 in English football"), under new manager [Tommy Coakley](/wiki/Tommy_Coakley "Tommy Coakley"), Walsall narrowly missed out on the play-offs but made considerable progress in the FA Cup as they defeated First Division Charlton Athletic and Birmingham City and took [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") to two replays in the fifth round.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Walsall_FC_League_Performance.svg/330px-Walsall_FC_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Walsall_FC_League_Performance.svg)

Chart of table positions of Walsall in the Football League.

Walsall finally earned promotion to the Second Division for the first time since 1963 by winning the Third Division [play-offs in 1988](/wiki/1988_Football_League_play-offs "1988 Football League play-offs"). [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") were beaten 4–0 in a replayed final at Fellows Park thanks to a [David Kelly](/wiki/David_Kelly_(association_footballer) "David Kelly (association footballer)") hattrick. However, the [1988–89 season](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_in_English_football "1988–89 in English football") saw the club immediately relegated from the Second Division and Ramsden's business empire collapse.[[13]](#cite_note-Ramsden-13) Walsall were minutes from going out of business but survived, again through the actions of the supporters and local businessmen. A second successive relegation followed at the end of [1989–90](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_in_English_football "1989–90 in English football") as Walsall were consigned to the Fourth Division once again.

The club moved to the [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium") in 1990.[[14]](#cite_note-Bescot-14) At the time it was a state-of-the-art arena and was only the second new Football League ground since the 1950s.[[14]](#cite_note-Bescot-14) The arrival at Bescot Stadium saw some stability brought back to the club after two successive relegations and the club was taken over by Jeff Bonser in 1991.[[15]](#cite_note-Bonser-15) [Kenny Hibbitt](/wiki/Kenny_Hibbitt "Kenny Hibbitt") managed the club for four years, setting the groundwork for a golden era for the club that would follow soon after his dismissal in September 1994.

New manager [Chris Nicholl](/wiki/Chris_Nicholl "Chris Nicholl") led the club to promotion back to the third tier (now known as Division Two after the formation of the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League")) in his first season, building the nucleus of a strong and under-rated team. A run of four straight wins at the end of April meant Walsall needed just a point from their final game, away to [Bury](/wiki/Bury_F.C. "Bury F.C."), to secure promotion; they duly obliged with a 0–0 scoreline to send the travelling fans home celebrating.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1): 66

### A series of ups and downs (1995–2022)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: A series of ups and downs (1995–2022)")]

Two seasons of stability followed back in Division Two before Nicholl resigned. [Jan Sørensen](/wiki/Jan_S%C3%B8rensen "Jan Sørensen") took the helm after Nicholl's departure and led the club to the fourth round of both the League Cup and FA Cup in [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_in_English_football "1997–98 in English football"). Each run was ended away to a Premier League side as [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") won 4–1 in the League Cup[[16]](#cite_note-16) and a glamour tie at [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") resulted in a 5–1 defeat in the FA Cup.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Despite the club's cup exploits, a poor finish in the league signalled the end of Sørensen's time at Walsall after just one season.

In [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_in_English_football "1998–99 in English football"), [Ray Graydon](/wiki/Ray_Graydon "Ray Graydon") took over as manager and led the club to a runners-up spot in Division Two, beating [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") to an automatic promotion place by five points.[[18]](#cite_note-Promotion_1999-18) After the unlikely promotion to the [second tier](/wiki/Football_League_Division_One "Football League Division One") Walsall found life difficult at a higher level but battled right until the final day of the season, when relegation was finally sealed. A 2–0 defeat at [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich_Town "Ipswich Town") meant Walsall returned to the third tier, despite derby wins over local rivals Tamworth, Rushall Olympic and [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") earlier in the campaign. *The Saddlers* returned to the second tier of English football at the first attempt, defeating [Reading](/wiki/Reading_FC "Reading FC") 3–2, after extra time, in a thrilling play-off final at Cardiff's [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium").[[19]](#cite_note-Promotion_2001-19)

Graydon was dismissed in January 2002 following a 2–0 defeat against local rivals West Brom. [Colin Lee](/wiki/Colin_Lee "Colin Lee") took over and secured survival in the second tier for the first time since the early 1960s. The [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football "2002–03 in English football") season saw Walsall avoid relegation again. However, the [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_in_English_football "2003–04 in English football") campaign ended in relegation despite a storming start which had seen the club on the brink of the play-offs going into the New Year. A slump in form saw Lee sacked in April[[20]](#cite_note-20) and the appointment of star player [Paul Merson](/wiki/Paul_Merson "Paul Merson") as manager did not halt the slide. Walsall were ultimately relegated, agonisingly by a single goal, despite a 3–2 victory over Rotherham United on the season's final day in front of a record Bescot Stadium crowd of 11,049.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Despite the club's relegation and no previous managerial experience, Merson was immediately appointed as full-time manager of the club in May 2004.[[22]](#cite_note-22) A poor season in [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") almost ended in successive relegations and the [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football "2005–06 in English football") season then turned into a disastrous one for Walsall and Merson. After increasing supporter pressure following a string of bad results, culminating in a 5–0 defeat at [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C."), Merson's reign as Walsall manager came to an end in February 2006.[[23]](#cite_note-23) However, it was too late and Walsall were relegated on 22 April 2006, after losing 3–1 to [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town "Huddersfield Town").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[Richard Money](/wiki/Richard_Money "Richard Money") was tasked with reviving the club's fortunes in [League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two"). An impressive start to the season was maintained throughout and, despite a mini-blip in February, Walsall remained in the top three for almost the entire season and were promoted back to [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") after beating [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County "Notts County") 2–1 away from home.[[25]](#cite_note-25) On the final day of the season Walsall drew 1–1 at [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town "Swindon Town"), thanks to a last-minute goal by [Dean Keates](/wiki/Dean_Keates "Dean Keates") in front of 3,419 travelling fans, to secure the [League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two") title.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/WalsallvGills2009.jpg/330px-WalsallvGills2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:WalsallvGills2009.jpg)

Walsall (in red shirts) playing [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.") in 2009 at Bescot Stadium

Walsall's form continued into the new season, as the club performed strongly in [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_in_English_football "2007–08 in English football"), including a run of 17 League matches without defeat and back-to-back promotions looked possible. However, a January transfer window that culminated in the sales of important first team players [Danny Fox](/wiki/Danny_Fox "Danny Fox") and [Scott Dann](/wiki/Scott_Dann "Scott Dann") to [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City "Coventry City")[[28]](#cite_note-28) caused a drop in form throughout 2008. The club's promotion challenge ended after a run of poor results in March leading to Richard Money resigning as manager in April.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The following seven seasons spent in League One saw largely mid-table security apart from a few flirtations with relegation. Notably, in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_in_English_football "2010–11 in English football") the club sat in the relegation places from October through to March but ultimately survived thanks to an upturn in form following the appointment of [Dean Smith](/wiki/Dean_Smith_(footballer,_born_1971) "Dean Smith (footballer, born 1971)") as manager in January 2011.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Walsall had been ten points adrift of safety, however, despite accumulating only 48 points by the end of the season they escaped relegation by one point.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_in_English_football "2014–15 in English football") was a memorable one for the club as it reached a [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") final for the first time in its 127-year history.[[12]](#cite_note-Wembley-12) Walsall beat [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_A.F.C. "Rochdale A.F.C."), [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers "Tranmere Rovers"), [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United "Sheffield United") and [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End "Preston North End") on their way to the [Football League Trophy final](/wiki/2015_Football_League_Trophy_Final "2015 Football League Trophy Final") where they were beaten 2–0 by Bristol City on Sunday 22 March 2015.[[32]](#cite_note-32) *The Saddlers* were backed by over 29,000 supporters in a crowd of 72,315 at the national stadium.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Walsall started the [2015–16 season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_in_English_football "2015–16 in English football") well, leading to interest in manager Dean Smith. At the end of November, with *The Saddlers* fourth in the table, he left Walsall for Brentford; at the time of his departure he was the fourth longest serving manager in the Football League.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Walsall turned to [Sean O'Driscoll](/wiki/Sean_O%27Driscoll "Sean O'Driscoll") to replace Smith.[[35]](#cite_note-35) However, after a six-game winless run and just 16 games in charge, O'Driscoll was sacked and replaced by [John Whitney](/wiki/Jon_Whitney_(footballer) "Jon Whitney (footballer)").[[36]](#cite_note-36) Ultimately, Walsall missed out on promotion by just one point and lost in the play-off semi-finals to Barnsley.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Following the decimation of their promotion challenging team, Walsall struggled for the next three seasons in League One and, after a disastrous spell in charge for former playing hero Dean Keates following the sacking of Whitney, were relegated back to the fourth tier at the end of the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_in_English_football "2018–19 in English football").[[38]](#cite_note-38) Following relegation, a new era began with [Darrell Clarke](/wiki/Darrell_Clarke "Darrell Clarke") taking over as manager[[39]](#cite_note-39) and Jeff Bonser ending his 28-year reign as owner and chairman by selling his 76% majority shareholding of the club to Leigh Pomlett in July 2019.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Walsall failed to make a League Two promotion challenge in the [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_in_English_football "2019–20 in English football") or [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_in_English_football "2020–21 in English football") seasons, finishing in 13th and 19th place respectively, halfway through the latter Clarke left for [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale "Port Vale"). Ahead of the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_in_English_football "2021–22 in English football") [Matthew Taylor](/wiki/Matthew_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1981) "Matthew Taylor (footballer, born 1981)") was appointed new head coach with [Neil McDonald](/wiki/Neil_McDonald_(footballer) "Neil McDonald (footballer)") as his assistant.[[41]](#cite_note-41) However, following a run of seven successive losses which left the club in 21st place and just four points outside the relegation zone, Taylor was sacked in February 2022 and replaced by [Michael Flynn](/wiki/Michael_Flynn_(footballer) "Michael Flynn (footballer)") who saved the club from relegation as they finished 16th.

### A new era of American ownership (2022–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: A new era of American ownership (2022–present)")]

On 6 June 2022, the club announced that American sports investment firm Trivela Group LLC had purchased a majority of shares belonging to chairman Leigh Pomlett, becoming 51% majority shareholders.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) A positive run of form in late 2022 saw the club within touching distance of the play-off places for the first time since relegation. However, the end of top goalscorer [Danny Johnson](/wiki/Danny_Johnson_(footballer) "Danny Johnson (footballer)")'s loan spell with the club in January led to an indifferent second half of the season. Ultimately, Flynn was sacked[[44]](#cite_note-44) as the club again finished 16th. Interim head coach [Mat Sadler](/wiki/Mat_Sadler "Mat Sadler") was appointed full-time head coach in May 2023, with Trivela stating their confidence that "Mat is the right man, given his values, his resolve, his ambition, and his abilities, to partner with us in building this future, and that he can continue to grow as a coach as we grow as a club."[[45]](#cite_note-45) Sadler led the team to a record breaking run of eight league wins in a row, securing the record with a 5–1 victory over [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers_F.C. "Tranmere Rovers F.C.") at the [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium") on 11 January 2025, before extending it the following week to nine.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-2025POF-47) Despite leading the league by 12 points and the play-off positions by 15 in January, a poor second half of the season saw Walsall miss out on automatic promotion on the final day of the season and subsequently lose the [play-off final](/wiki/2025_EFL_League_Two_play-off_final "2025 EFL League Two play-off final") 1–0 to [AFC Wimbledon](/wiki/AFC_Wimbledon "AFC Wimbledon").[[47]](#cite_note-2025POF-47) On 11 March 2026, with the club in 11th place, Sadler was sacked with his assistant [Darren Byfield](/wiki/Darren_Byfield "Darren Byfield") taking charge for the remainder of the season.

## Rivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rivals")]

A 2013 survey revealed Walsall fans consider Black Country neighbours [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers "Wolverhampton Wanderers") to be the club's main rivals.[[48]](#cite_note-48) However, meetings between the teams are relatively rare, with Wolves having spent most of their existence in the top two tiers of English football. Only [16 competitive fixtures](/wiki/Black_Country_derby "Black Country derby") have been played between Walsall and Wolves, with the most recent occurring in 2014.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Meetings with the Black Country's other professional club, [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion "West Bromwich Albion"), are similarly rare, with *The Saddlers* and *The Baggies* having clashed just 14 times.[[50]](#cite_note-50) However, the Black Country rivals did meet on numerous occasions during wartime regionalised league fixtures.[[1]](#cite_note-HistoryBook-1) A match between two of those three clubs is called [Black Country derby](/wiki/Black_Country_derby "Black Country derby").

More regularly-contested rivalries exist with [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town "Shrewsbury Town") and [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale "Port Vale"), who are often *The Saddlers'* geographically closest league fixtures. Walsall have the upper hand in the Shrewsbury and Port Vale rivalries, having won significantly more fixtures than they have lost.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Grounds")]

### The Chuckery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Chuckery")]

This multi-purpose sports ground was situated in a district near to the Walsall Arboretum. It comprised some 12 football pitches and four good-sized cricket squares. It was the first ever home ground for Walsall F.C. from 1875 until 1893.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### West Bromwich Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: West Bromwich Road")]

The new ground in West Bromwich Road, which had a capacity of just over 4,500, proved to be a lucky omen for The Saddlers between 1893 and 1896.

### Fellows Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fellows Park")]

Main article: [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park")

Fellows Park was a former football stadium in Walsall, England. It was the home ground of Walsall F.C. from 1896 until 1990, when the team moved to the [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium").

### Bescot Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bescot Stadium")]

Main article: [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium")

Bescot Stadium, currently also known as the Pallet-Track Bescot Stadium for sponsorship purposes,[[55]](#cite_note-Walsall's_home_becomes_Pallet-Track_Bescot_Stadium_as_part_of_new_partnership-55) is the home ground of Walsall Football Club. It was built in 1989–90 at a cost of £4.5m, replacing the club's previous ground, [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park"), which was located a quarter of a mile away. The ground was opened by [Sir Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Sir_Stanley_Matthews "Sir Stanley Matthews"). Upon Jeff Bonser stepping down from his role at the club, Leigh Pomlett agreed an option to reunite the Saddlers with their stadium freehold "in due time". On 16 December 2022 the club executed its option to acquire Poundland Bescot Stadium and is now the owner of the stadium, the Saddlers Club, and the adjoining land. On Saturday 27 January 2024 the Supporters Club, formerly known as the Saddlers Club, re-opened as a sports bar under the new name The Locker.

## Club records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walsall_F.C.&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Club records")]

**Competitions**

* Highest ever [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") position: **6th**, [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") [1898–99](/wiki/1898%E2%80%9399_in_English_football "1898–99 in English football")
* Highest post-war [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") position: **14th**, [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") [1961–62](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_in_English_football "1961–62 in English football")
* [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup"): **5th Round**, [1938–39](/wiki/1938%E2%80%9339_FA_Cup "1938–39 FA Cup"), [1974–75](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_FA_Cup "1974–75 FA Cup"), [1977–78](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_FA_Cup "1977–78 FA Cup"), [1986–87](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_FA_Cup "1986–87 FA Cup"), [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_FA_Cup "2001–02 FA Cup"), [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_FA_Cup "2002–03 FA Cup")
* [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup"): **Semi-final**, [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_Football_League_Cup "1983–84 Football League Cup")

**Scores**

* League Win: **10–0** vs. [Darwen](/wiki/Darwen_F.C._(1870) "Darwen F.C. (1870)"). Second Division, 4 March 1899
* League Defeat: **0–12** vs. [Small Heath](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."). Second Division, 17 December 1892
* Cup Win: **12–0** vs. [Warmley](/wiki/Warmley_F.C. "Warmley F.C."). FA Cup 1st Qualifying Round, 27 September 1890
* Cup Defeat: **0–7** vs. [Worcester City](/wiki/Worcester_City_F.C. "Worcester City F.C."). FA Cup 1st Qualifying Round, 11 October 1913

**Sequences**

* Most League Games Won in a Row (**9**): 2024–25
* Most League Games Lost in a Row (**15**): 1988–89
* Most League Games without Defeat (**21**): 1979–80
* Most League Games without Victory (**18**): 1988–89

**Attendances**

* Highest League Attendance (at [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park")): **25,453** v. [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."). Second Division (now Championship), 29 August 1961
* Highest League Attendance (at [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium")): **11,049** v. [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C."). First Division (now Championship), 9 May 2004
* Highest Third Division (now League One) Attendance: **19,589** v. [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C."), 18 March 1950
* Highest Fourth Division (now League Two) Attendance: **15,403** v. [Carlisle United](/wiki/Carlisle_United_F.C. "Carlisle United F.C."), 10 September 1959
* Highest [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") Attendance: **24,045** v. [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C."), 4th Round Replay, 30 January 1962
* Highest [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") Attendance: **21,066** v. [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). 4th Round, 17 February 1968
* Highest [Football League Trophy](/wiki/Football_League_Trophy "Football League Trophy") Attendance: **10,038** v. [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C."). Area Final Second Leg, 27 January 2015
* Highest Average Attendance (at [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park")): **15,711**, 1947–48
* Highest Average Attendance (at [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium")): **7,853**, 2003–04

**Players**

* Most Appearances: **534** – [Jimmy Walker](/wiki/Jimmy_Walker_(footballer,_born_1973) "Jimmy Walker (footballer, born 1973)") 1993–04, 2010–13
* Most League Appearances: **473** – [Colin Harrison](/wiki/Colin_Harrison_(footballer) "Colin Harrison (footballer)") 1964–82
* Top Goalscorer: **202** – [Alan Buckley](/wiki/Alan_Buckley "Alan Buckley") 1973–85
* Top League Goalscorer: **185** – [Tony Richards](/wiki/Tony_Richards_(footballer,_born_1934) "Tony Richards (footballer, born 1934)") 1954–63
* Most League Goals in a Season: **39** – [Gilbert Alsop](/wiki/Gilbert_Alsop "Gilbert Alsop") 1933–34 and 1934–35
* Most capped player: **19** [![Guyana](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Flag_of_Guyana.svg/40px-Flag_of_Guyana.svg.png)](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana") [Liam Gordon](/wiki/Liam_Gordon_(footballer,_born_1999) "Liam Gordon (footballer, born 1999)") 2023–25
* Highest Transfer Fee Paid: **£270,000** (€300,000) – for [Andreas Makris](/wiki/Andreas_Makris_(footballer) "Andreas Makris (footballer)") to [Anorthosis Famagusta](/wiki/Anorthosis_Famagusta_FC "Anorthosis Famagusta FC"), August 2016[[109]](#cite_note-118)[[110]](#cite_note-119)
* Highest Transfer Fee Received: **£1,500,000** – for [Rico Henry](/wiki/Rico_Henry "Rico Henry") from [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C."), August 2016[[111]](#cite_note-120)
